title: Twice-Exceptional Students: When Giftedness and Learning Disabilities Overlap
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your child has an extraordinary vocabulary and can discuss complex ideas with adults. They also cannot write a coherent paragraph to save their life. They ace every math reasoning problem but fail the timed multiplication tests. They are clearly brilliant. They are clearly struggling. The contradiction confuses everyone, including your child, who has concluded that they must be lazy or broken. They are neither. They may be twice-exceptional, a term that describes students who are both gifted and learning-disabled. These students are among the most underserved in education because their strengths mask their weaknesses and their weaknesses mask their strengths. The result is often a child who performs at grade level and appears average while possessing extraordinary abilities and extraordinary needs. Here is what twice-exceptionality means and how to support a child who lives it.

Why Twice-Exceptional Students Are So Often Missed

The identification of twice-exceptional students is complicated by the compensating effect of their strengths. A child with dyslexia who has a remarkable memory may memorize entire books and appear to be reading fluently, when in fact they are reciting from memory rather than decoding the words. A child with ADHD who has exceptional reasoning ability may perform well on untimed assessments that allow them to think deeply, while failing timed tasks that require sustained attention and processing speed. The giftedness compensates for the disability, pulling performance up to the average range. The disability masks the giftedness, pulling performance down from the exceptional range. The result is a child who looks average on paper and is struggling far more than anyone realizes.

Teachers and parents often misinterpret what they see. The child who writes poorly but speaks brilliantly is accused of not trying. The child who cannot memorize math facts but solves complex problems is told they just need to apply themselves. The message the child receives is that they are choosing to fail. The reality is that they are working harder than most children to produce average results, and the effort is invisible to the adults around them.

The identification process itself creates barriers. Gifted programs often require high standardized test scores, which twice-exceptional students may not achieve because of their disabilities. Special education evaluations often require evidence of academic failure, which twice-exceptional students may not demonstrate because of their giftedness. The child falls through the gap between the two systems, qualifying for neither the enrichment they need nor the support they need.

What Twice-Exceptionality Looks Like

Twice-exceptionality is not a single profile. It is a category that includes many different combinations of giftedness and disability. A child can be gifted in verbal reasoning and have dyslexia. Gifted in mathematics and have ADHD. Gifted in spatial reasoning and have an autism spectrum disorder. Gifted in creative thinking and have a processing speed deficit. Each combination produces a different pattern of strengths and challenges.

Despite the diversity of profiles, twice-exceptional students often share common experiences. They feel like they do not fit anywhere, not smart enough for the gifted program, not struggling enough for special education. They have areas of extreme ability and areas of extreme difficulty, with little in between. They are intensely frustrated by their own inconsistency. They may have developed elaborate coping strategies that hide their difficulties from adults. They may have stopped trying in their areas of weakness because the effort-to-outcome ratio is so discouraging.

Asynchronous development is typical. A child may be reading at a high school level and managing emotions at a preschool level. The intellectual age and the emotional age are far apart. Adults often expect the child to be as mature as they are smart, and are frustrated when the child behaves like the child they developmentally are. The child learns that their emotions are unacceptable and tries to suppress them, which works until it does not.

How to Support a Twice-Exceptional Child

Focus on strengths first. The twice-exceptional child has spent years feeling deficient. They need opportunities to experience mastery and to see themselves as capable. Provide enrichment in their areas of giftedness. If they are verbally gifted, give them access to advanced books and sophisticated conversation. If they are mathematically gifted, provide challenging problems that engage their reasoning ability. The enrichment is not a reward for good behavior. It is a necessary counterbalance to the daily experience of struggle in their areas of disability.

Address the disability directly and without shame. If your child has dyslexia, they need explicit, systematic reading instruction. If they have ADHD, they may benefit from medication, behavioral strategies, or both. If they have a writing disability, they need access to assistive technology like speech-to-text software. The disability is not something to overcome through sheer effort. It is a neurological difference that requires specific accommodations and interventions. Providing those interventions communicates that the disability is real and that your child deserves support.

Teach your child about their own brain. A child who understands that they have a fast brain for reasoning and a slow brain for processing, or a strong brain for ideas and a weak brain for writing, can make sense of their experience. The explanation reduces shame and builds self-awareness. The child who knows their own profile can advocate for what they need and can pursue careers that align with their strengths rather than being limited by their weaknesses.

Advocate for dual services. Your child may qualify for both gifted education and special education services. The combination is rare but possible. An Individualized Education Program can include both enrichment goals and support goals. The school may resist this combination because it is administratively unusual. Persist. Your child needs both the challenge that engages their giftedness and the support that addresses their disability. Providing only one or the other will not meet their needs.

The Bottom Line

Twice-exceptional students are both gifted and learning-disabled. Their strengths and weaknesses mask each other, leaving them underserved and often unidentified. Focus on developing their strengths while providing specific support for their disabilities. Teach them about their own cognitive profile. Advocate for dual services that address both their giftedness and their learning needs. The goal is not to fix the child. It is to help them understand and work with the brain they have.
